data.sentence: Hi Jason, thanks for joining — this is Sarah Chen from Aegis support, I'll be handling case number 1514 for you today.
Hey Sarah, yeah thanks for getting on a call, I appreciate it — email threads on this kind of thing just go in circles.

Totally understand, it's a lot easier to just talk through it directly. So I pulled up the case before hopping on — looks like you're looking at granular restore capabilities within Aegis Protect, is that the right starting point?

Yeah, exactly. So the short version is — we had an incident internally, nothing catastrophic, but one of our analysts accidentally overwrote a set of config files in our trading platform environment, and we needed to restore just those specific files, not the whole VM or the whole volume.
Okay, got it. And when you went into Protect to try and do that, what did you run into?

So, the restore options we're seeing are basically — restore the full snapshot, or restore at the volume level. There's no way that I can find to, uh, drill down and say I want this specific directory, or these three files from this snapshot point.

Right, yeah, so that's — that's actually an accurate read of where the product is today. Granular file-level restore, like truly arbitrary path-based restore from within a snapshot, that's not currently a supported workflow in Protect.
Okay. I mean, I kind of suspected that was going to be the answer but I wanted to confirm it rather than assume.
No, absolutely right to ask. So let me just — can I ask a few more questions about the specific scenario so I can make sure I capture this accurately for the feature request ticket?
Sure, go ahead.
So the workload in question — is this an EC2 instance, a VM in a private cloud environment, or something else?
It's EC2, yeah. We're running on CloudPrime, the trading platform environment specifically is in us-east-1. The config files in question are on an EBS volume attached to the instance.
Okay, EBS-backed EC2, us-east-1. And the snapshots are being managed through Protect — so you've got a backup policy applied to that instance?

Correct, we've got an hourly snapshot policy on that environment. So the snapshots exist, they're there, it's just — we can't get to individual files without restoring the whole volume, which is kind of a nuclear option for what we actually need.

Yeah, that makes complete sense. Restoring a full volume just for a handful of config files is a lot of overhead, especially if the instance needs to stay up or there are dependency concerns. In this case, how did you ultimately resolve it — were you able to get the files back another way?

Yeah, we ended up spinning up a temporary EC2 instance, restoring the volume to that, mounting it, pulling the files manually, and then copying them back. It worked but it was, uh, it was probably a two-hour process for something that should have taken ten minutes.

Yeah, that's a pretty painful workaround. I want to be transparent with you — that manual approach is essentially what the product forces today if you need file-level granularity, and I completely acknowledge that's not a great experience.

Right. And look, for a financial services environment, uh, we have pretty strict RTO requirements on certain systems. A two-hour workaround process isn't something I can count on operationally. It kind of worked this time but it's not a repeatable procedure I'd want to rely on.
Understood. And that's exactly the kind of context that's valuable to document. Can I ask — what kind of RTO are you targeting for this class of restore, roughly?

For the trading platform environment specifically, we're targeting fifteen minutes or under for file-level restores. Obviously full system recovery has a different SLA, but for — you know, config corruption or an accidental overwrite scenario — fifteen minutes is kind of our threshold.

Fifteen minutes, got that. And are we talking about, like, structured file types — configs, CSVs, that kind of thing — or do you also need to think about database-level granularity, like individual records or tables?

For now, the ask is really around file-level. Flat files, config files, maybe log archives in some cases. Database granularity is a whole other conversation, we manage that separately through our DBA team with native tooling.

Perfect, that actually simplifies things from a scoping standpoint. So I'm going to log this as a formal feature request — granular file-level restore for EBS-backed workloads, with the ability to browse the snapshot contents and select specific paths or directories for restore. Does that capture what you're looking for?

Yeah, that's the core of it. I'd also add — the ability to restore to an alternate location would be useful. So not necessarily back to the original path, but maybe restore to a staging directory so we can validate before we overwrite anything in production.
Oh, that's a good addition — restore to alternate path, so you can do a staged recovery rather than directly overwriting in place. I'll include that. Anything else that comes to mind?

Um — I mean, the ability to preview the file before restoring it would be nice. Like, just a basic metadata view, see the file size, timestamp, confirm it's the right version before you commit. But I'd call that a nice-to-have, not a blocker.

Got it, I'll flag that as a secondary ask — snapshot file browser with metadata preview. That's actually come up on a couple other accounts so it's good to have it documented with your name attached to it.
Sure. And just so I understand the process — what happens with a feature request like this? Does it just go into a backlog somewhere, or is there some kind of review cycle?

So, the way it works is — I submit this into our product feedback system, it gets triaged by the Protect product team, and they assess it based on request volume, impact, and technical feasibility. High-priority items from enterprise accounts like yours do tend to get escalated for roadmap consideration, but I can't give you a commitment on timeline.
That's fair. I'm not expecting a date, I just want to know it's not going into a black hole.

It won't — you'll have a case number attached to this, and I'll make sure your CSM gets looped in so there's a human who can give you updates on it. Speaking of which, do you want me to copy anyone specific at Meridian on the case documentation?
Yeah, if you could copy Marcus Webb on my team — he's the one who actually went through the whole workaround process, so he'll want visibility into any updates.
Marcus Webb, got it, I'll add him to the case. And just to close the loop — is there anything else on the immediate side that's blocking you, or is this more of a forward-looking ask at this point?
It's forward-looking. The immediate incident is resolved, we're not down or anything. It's more — I don't want to be in that situation again and have the same painful workaround be my only option.

Completely reasonable. Alright, so to summarize — I've got the feature request documented: granular file-level restore from EBS snapshots, restore to alternate path support, and file metadata preview as a secondary ask. Case 1514, I'll get Marcus added, and I'll loop in your CSM for follow-up. Does that cover everything?
Yeah, that covers it. Thanks for being straightforward about where the product is today — I'd rather have an honest answer than get told it's coming soon and then wait a year.

Yeah, I think that's the only way to have a useful conversation. Alright, Jason — I'll get the case updated today and you should see the documentation in your portal within the hour. Thanks for your time.
Sounds good, thanks Sarah.
